questions.question: Why are values-based boundary goals more sustainable than rule-based or 'should'-driven goals?

options.text: They're not—all boundary goals are equally effective
Values-based goals are rooted in intrinsic motivation (what YOU care about), making them self-renewing. 'Should'-based goals rely on external pressure or willpower, which collapses under stress.
Values-based goals are easier so they require less effort
Only therapists can create values-based goals

questions.explanation: Research shows values-based goals predict sustained behavior change because they're intrinsically motivated—you WANT to honor your values, not just comply with a rule. Example: 'I value rest, so I protect evenings' (sustainable) vs. 'I should work less because my therapist said so' (collapses under pressure). Values are self-renewing; 'shoulds' require willpower.

questions.question: What does the research say about trying to change multiple behaviors simultaneously?

options.text: The more behaviors you change at once, the better
Trying to change too many behaviors simultaneously predicts failure—better to focus on 1-3 specific goals, build success and confidence, then add more
You should only ever work on one goal at a time
Behavior change research doesn't apply to boundaries

questions.explanation: Behavior change research shows attempting too many changes at once leads to overwhelm and failure. Better approach: Focus on 1-3 specific boundary goals in your weakest domains, build success and momentum, then add more. Small wins build self-efficacy. Start with what feels most urgent OR most achievable, depending on your capacity.

questions.question: What is 'barrier planning' and why does it predict success?

options.text: Barrier planning means avoiding all obstacles

Barrier planning means identifying likely obstacles (internal: guilt, fear, trauma; external: others' reactions, constraints) and preparing responses in advance—it predicts success because you're not surprised when obstacles arise
Barrier planning is only for people with anxiety
You should wait until barriers appear before planning responses

questions.explanation: Barrier planning means anticipating obstacles (guilt, fear, others' pushback, financial constraints, trauma triggers) and planning your response in advance. Example: 'If my boss emails after 7pm, I'll use broken record technique.' This predicts success because you're prepared—you don't have to make decisions in the moment when you're stressed or triggered.

questions.question: If you recognized in Lesson 19 that your boundary difficulties are rooted in trauma, what should be your first priority in your boundary plan?

options.text: Set the hardest boundaries first to prove you're healing
Seek trauma therapy first—boundary skills become accessible AFTER processing. Start with easiest boundary goals to build safety. Practice nervous system regulation. Use self-compassion, not shame.
Avoid all boundary work until trauma is completely healed
Push through trauma triggers by forcing yourself to set boundaries

questions.explanation: If boundary difficulties are trauma-rooted, your plan should prioritize: (1) Seek trauma therapy (EMDR, somatic experiencing, IFS, etc.)—this is the foundation, (2) Practice nervous system regulation before difficult conversations, (3) Start with EASIEST boundary goals to build safety and success, (4) Self-compassion, not shame. Boundary skills become accessible after processing—don't shame yourself for needing therapy first. That IS the boundary work.

questions.question: What is the most important thing to remember when you fail at maintaining a boundary?

options.text: You should give up on boundary work because you're bad at it
Boundary failures are normal and repairable. Use self-compassionate repair: Notice without shame → Identify why → Recommit → 'I'm learning. I'm not starting over—I'm continuing.'
You should shame yourself so you don't fail again
One failure means all your progress is erased

questions.explanation: THE MOST CRITICAL TAKEAWAY: Boundary failures are NORMAL. Everyone fails. The skill is in self-compassionate repair: (1) Notice without shame, (2) Identify why it happened, (3) Recommit to the boundary, (4) Self-compassion: 'I'm learning. I'm not starting over—I'm continuing.' Shame causes people to abandon boundary work entirely. Self-compassion allows you to recommit and keep growing.

title: Living with Boundaries — Your Personal Plan Builder - Quiz
